Getting Started with HTTP Caching

HTTP caching reduces latency and load by reusing previously fetched resources. Caches live in browsers, proxies, and CDNs. Understanding three primitives — freshness, validation, and invalidation — covers almost every real-world scenario.

Freshness

A response is fresh until it expires. The server signals freshness with Cache-Control: max-age=N or, less commonly, an absolute Expires header. When a cached response is fresh, the cache serves it directly with zero network round-trips. This is the fastest possible path and the reason caching exists.

Validation

Once a response is stale, the cache must validate it before reuse. Validation typically uses ETag (an opaque versioned identifier) or Last-Modified (a timestamp). The cache sends a conditional request — If-None-Match or If-Modified-Since — and the server either returns 304 Not Modified (reuse) or a fresh 200 OK with a new body.

Invalidation

Unsafe methods (POST, PUT, DELETE) invalidate cache entries for the affected URL. This prevents staleness when state changes. The exact rules vary; the safe assumption is that any state-mutating request invalidates its target.

Practical advice

Set Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable on fingerprinted static assets. Use short max-age with stale-while-revalidate for HTML. Always include Vary when responses depend on request headers like Accept-Encoding or Cookie.
